subject: Governance
trap_factor: Margaret Atwood also won the Booker Prize (2000) and is associated with it — but J.M. Coetzee won it twice (1983 for Life & Times of Michael K; 1999 for Disgrace), making B the answer.
elimination_hint: J.M. Coetzee is the only author to have won the Man Booker Prize twice (1983, 1999); he also won the Nobel Prize in Literature (2003).
factual_hooks: J.M. Coetzee won the Booker Prize in 1983 (Life & Times of Michael K) and 1999 (Disgrace)
Hilary Mantel also won it twice (2009, 2012) but was not an option
Margaret Atwood won once (2000, The Blind Assassin)
contrast_with: Booker Prize vs Nobel Prize for Literature

subject: History
trap_factor: Pair 2 places Varaha Avatar sculpture at Mount Abu — it is actually at Udayagiri Caves (Madhya Pradesh). Ajanta has the Mahaparinirvana image and Mamallapuram has Arjuna's Penance.
elimination_hint: The colossal Varaha (boar incarnation of Vishnu) relief is at Udayagiri Caves, Vidisha, MP — not Mount Abu (which is known for Dilwara Jain temples). Pairs 1 and 3 are correct.
factual_hooks: Ajanta Cave 26 has the famous Mahaparinirvana sculpture
Arjuna's Penance/Descent of Ganga is a massive bas-relief at Mamallapuram
Udayagiri Caves (MP) have the famous Varaha Avatar sculpture — not Mount Abu
contrast_with: Ajanta (Buddhist) vs Udayagiri (Hindu Gupta) vs Mamallapuram (Pallava)

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Ghadar with the Berlin India Committee or Home Rule League — Ghadar was specifically a San Francisco-based revolutionary movement for Indian independence.
elimination_hint: Ghadar Party was founded in 1913 by Lala Har Dayal and others in San Francisco, USA — a revolutionary overseas Indian movement targeting British rule.
factual_hooks: Ghadar Party was founded in 1913 at San Francisco by Lala Har Dayal
It published the revolutionary newspaper 'Ghadar' (Revolution)
It sought to incite mutiny in the Indian Army during WWI
contrast_with: Ghadar (San Francisco) vs Indian Independence League (Southeast Asia) vs Berlin Committee (Germany)

subject: History
trap_factor: Options A and C suggest it is a religious cult or dance-drama — Kalaripayattu is purely a martial art from Kerala, not a bhakti cult or performance art.
elimination_hint: Kalaripayattu is one of the world's oldest martial arts, originating in Kerala — a living tradition of unarmed and armed combat techniques.
factual_hooks: Kalaripayattu is an ancient martial art from Kerala
It is considered one of the oldest martial arts in the world
It influences classical dance forms like Kathakali through body movement training
contrast_with: Kalaripayattu (Kerala martial art) vs Gatka (Punjab) vs Silambam (Tamil Nadu)
contemporary_relevance: Kalaripayattu is gaining global recognition and is proposed for UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage listing.

subject: History
trap_factor: Mohiniattam is from Kerala, not Odisha (Odisha is associated with Odissi dance) — students may confuse these two classical forms from eastern and southern coastal regions.
elimination_hint: Garba-Gujarat and Yakshagana-Karnataka are correct. Mohiniattam belongs to Kerala, not Odisha. Answer is C (pairs 1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Garba is the folk/classical dance of Gujarat
Yakshagana is a traditional theatre form of Karnataka
Mohiniattam is a classical dance from Kerala — NOT Odisha (Odissi is from Odisha)
prerequisite_concepts: Classical and folk dance forms of India and their states
contrast_with: Mohiniattam (Kerala) vs Odissi (Odisha)

subject: History
trap_factor: Pair 2 places Lhotsava Lhakhang (Nako) in Zanskar Valley — it is actually in Spiti/Kinnaur area (Himachal Pradesh), not Zanskar Valley (which is in Ladakh/J&K). Only pairs 1 and 3 are correct.
elimination_hint: Tabo monastery is in Spiti Valley (HP) — correct. Nako village with Lhotsava Lhakhang is also in HP's Kinnaur district, not Zanskar Valley. Alchi is in Ladakh — correct. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Tabo Monastery (996 CE) is in Spiti Valley, Himachal Pradesh — one of the oldest Buddhist monasteries
Alchi Temple Complex is in Ladakh — known for Kashmiri-influenced Buddhist murals
Nako's Lhotsava Lhakhang is in Kinnaur (HP), not Zanskar Valley
contrast_with: Spiti Valley (HP) vs Zanskar Valley (Ladakh)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Bijak is of Dadu Dayal — it is actually of Kabir. Statement 2 says Pushti Marg was by Madhvacharya — it was by Vallabhacharya. Both statements are wrong.
elimination_hint: Bijak is the sacred text of Kabir's teachings, not Dadu Dayal. Pushti Marg (devotional path to Krishna) was founded by Vallabhacharya, not Madhvacharya (who founded Dvaita Vedanta). Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Bijak is the primary text of Kabir's teachings
Pushti Marg was founded by Vallabhacharya (not Madhvacharya)
Madhvacharya founded Dvaita Vedanta and Brahma Sampradaya
contrast_with: Vallabhacharya (Pushti Marg/Shuddhadvaita) vs Madhvacharya (Dvaita Vedanta)

subject: History
trap_factor: Manganiyars are often confused with classical musicians — they are hereditary folk musicians from the Thar Desert region of Rajasthan (northwest India), known for their traditional music.
elimination_hint: Manganiyars are Muslim hereditary folk musicians from Rajasthan (northwest India) — not martial artists, not classical vocalists, not pietra dura craftsmen. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Manganiyars are hereditary folk musicians from Barmer-Jaisalmer region of Rajasthan
They perform for Rajput and other patron communities
Known for their distinctive sarangi and khartal playing
contrast_with: Manganiyars (folk, Rajasthan) vs Dhrupad (classical, court music)
contemporary_relevance: Manganiyars have gained global recognition through collaborations and world music festivals.

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may think Ibadat Khana was a mosque or prayer room — it was actually Akbar's hall of religious discourse where scholars of different faiths debated.
elimination_hint: Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) at Fatehpur Sikri was built by Akbar in 1575 for inter-religious discussions — not a private prayer chamber or mosque.
factual_hooks: Ibadat Khana was established by Akbar in 1575 at Fatehpur Sikri
Initially only for Muslim scholars; later opened to Hindus, Jains, Christians, Zoroastrians
Reflects Akbar's syncretic religious policy and Din-i-Ilahi
contrast_with: Ibadat Khana (inter-faith debate) vs Diwan-i-Khas (select audience) vs Diwan-i-Am (public audience)
contemporary_relevance: Akbar's religious pluralism is often cited as a model for managing diversity.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: This 2004-era question tests specific officeholder knowledge — G. Madhavan Nair was ISRO chairman; Anil Kakodar was chairman of AEC; positions for Raman Puri and M. Jagannatha Rao need careful recall.
elimination_hint: G. Madhavan Nair was ISRO Chairman (not AEC). Anil Kakodar was AEC chairman. Cross-check: B gives A-4 (Kakodar-AEC), B-2 (Puri-Law Commission), C-1 (Jagannatha Rao-Integrated Defence), D-3 (Nair-ISRO) — this is correct.
factual_hooks: G. Madhavan Nair served as ISRO Chairman from 2003-2009
Anil Kakodar served as Chairman of Atomic Energy Commission
This was a current affairs question from 2004
prerequisite_concepts: Key scientific and administrative positions in India

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 is wrong — India does have a National Seeds Policy (2002). Statement 2 is wrong — private sector extensively supplies vegetable/horticultural seeds. Only statement 3 (demand-supply gap for low-value high-volume crops) is a real constraint.
elimination_hint: National Seeds Policy 2002 exists; private sector is very active in vegetable seeds. The genuine constraint is statement 3 — quality seeds for staple crops (paddy, wheat) face demand-supply gaps. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: India has a National Seeds Policy (2002)
Seed Replacement Rate (SRR) is low for staple crops like paddy and wheat
Private sector dominates hybrid vegetable seed supply but neglects low-margin staple crops
contrast_with: High SRR crops (vegetables, hybrids) vs low SRR crops (paddy, wheat)
contemporary_relevance: Improving SRR for staple crops is critical for achieving food production targets.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 1 says ESZs are declared under Wildlife Protection Act — they are actually declared under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. Statement 2 says all activities are prohibited — they are only regulated, not prohibited. Both are wrong.
elimination_hint: Eco-Sensitive Zones are notified under the Environment (Protection) Act 1986, not Wildlife Protection Act. They regulate (not prohibit) activities — mining may be banned but tourism and agriculture continue. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: ESZs are notified under Environment (Protection) Act 1986, Sec 3(2)(v)
The purpose is to regulate, not prohibit, human activity in the buffer zone
Supreme Court mandated 10km ESZ around Protected Areas in a 2022 ruling (later modified)
contrast_with: Core zone (no human activity) vs ESZ (regulated activity)
contemporary_relevance: The SC ESZ ruling of 2022 sparked controversy in tribal/forest-dwelling communities regarding livelihood restrictions.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Animal Welfare Board is under Environment Protection Act — it is actually under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act 1960. Statements 2 and 3 are correct.
elimination_hint: AWBI is under PCA Act 1960 (not EPA 1986). NTCA is a statutory body under Wildlife Protection Act 1972 (Amendment 2006). NGRBA is chaired by PM. Answer is B (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) established under Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960
National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) is a statutory body under WPA 1972
National Ganga River Basin Authority was chaired by the Prime Minister
prerequisite_concepts: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act
contrast_with: AWBI (PCA Act) vs NTCA (WPA) vs NGRBA (Environment Protection Act)
contemporary_relevance: NTCA's role in tiger population recovery is globally recognised as a conservation success.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: World Economic Forum (Davos) publishes Global Competitiveness Report — students may confuse WEF with IMF for economic outlook publications.
elimination_hint: World Economic Outlook is IMF's flagship publication released twice yearly — not to be confused with World Development Report (World Bank) or Human Development Report (UNDP).
factual_hooks: World Economic Outlook is published by IMF (twice a year)
World Bank publishes World Development Report
UNDP publishes Human Development Report
related_concepts: World Economic Forum Global Risks Report
contrast_with: IMF (WEO) vs World Bank (WDR) vs UNDP (HDR) publications
contemporary_relevance: IMF's World Economic Outlook projections directly influence investor sentiment and policy decisions globally.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Subsidies may seem like 'plan expenditure' for welfare programmes, but in budget classification, defence, interest, salaries, pensions, and subsidies all fall under Non-Plan Expenditure (now Revenue/Capital under revised classification).
elimination_hint: All four — defence, interest payments, salaries/pensions, subsidies — are Non-Plan Expenditure. Non-Plan expenditure covers committed liabilities of the government. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Non-Plan expenditure includes defence, interest payments, subsidies, salaries/pensions
Plan expenditure was for Five-Year Plan allocations (now replaced by capital/revenue classification)
India discontinued the Plan/Non-Plan distinction from 2017-18 budget
contrast_with: Plan expenditure (developmental) vs Non-Plan (committed/obligatory)
contemporary_relevance: Post-2017 India merged Plan and Non-Plan into Capital and Revenue heads for better fiscal clarity.

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: Intra-generational equity concerns fair distribution among people living today
Inter-generational equity means not compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs (Brundtland definition)
Both concepts are foundational to SDGs and sustainable development frameworks
contrast_with: Intra-generational equity (present people) vs Inter-generational equity (future vs present)
mains_intro: Equitable development requires addressing both the disparities among people alive today (intra-generational equity) and the moral obligation to preserve resources and opportunities for future generations (inter-generational equity).
mains_body_points: Intra-generational equity: reducing inequality within the current generation — between rich and poor, urban and rural, across genders and regions; SDG 10 (reduced inequalities) directly addresses this
Inter-generational equity: development today should not deplete natural capital, pollute environments, or create debt burdens that compromise future generations' quality of life
Linkage with inclusive growth: growth must be broad-based, generating employment and reducing poverty (intra) while being environmentally sustainable (inter)
India's challenges: intra — SC/ST/OBC/women gaps; inter — groundwater depletion, soil degradation, unsustainable fiscal deficits
Policy instruments: progressive taxation, green budgeting, carbon pricing, social protection floors balance both equity dimensions
mains_conclusion: Inclusive growth demands simultaneous attention to present-day disparities and long-term sustainability, making these two equity dimensions complementary rather than competing priorities.
contemporary_relevance: India's commitment to Net Zero by 2070 and SDG targets embodies both intra- and inter-generational equity imperatives.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: This is a 1996 question about which literary work was recently adapted to film — Upamanyu Chatterjee's 'English August' was adapted to film in 1994.
elimination_hint: English August (1994 film) by Dev Benegal based on Upamanyu Chatterjee's novel was the most recent film adaptation at the time of the 1996 question.
factual_hooks: English August (novel 1988) was adapted to film in 1994
Directed by Dev Benegal, it depicted an IAS officer's experience in rural India

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse 'The Indian Struggle' with Lajpat Rai — it is Subhash Chandra Bose's autobiography. Lajpat Rai wrote 'Autobiographical Writings', not Bose.
elimination_hint: Gandhi wrote Hind Swaraj; Bose wrote The Indian Struggle; Surendranath Banerjee wrote A Nation in Making; Lajpat Rai wrote Autobiographical Writings. Answer: I-D, II-A, III-B, IV-C.
factual_hooks: Surendranath Banerjee: A Nation in Making (autobiography)
Gandhi: Hind Swaraj (political treatise on Indian self-rule)
Subhash Chandra Bose: The Indian Struggle (history of Indian independence movement)
Lajpat Rai: Autobiographical Writings
contrast_with: Hind Swaraj (political vision) vs The Indian Struggle (historical account)

subject: Science & Technology
factual_hooks: Nanotechnology operates at 1-100 nanometre scale
Nanomedicine enables targeted drug delivery, reducing side effects
Nanoparticles used in cancer therapy, diagnostic imaging, wound dressings
contrast_with: Conventional drug delivery vs nano drug delivery (targeted vs systemic)
mains_intro: Nanotechnology, operating at the scale of individual atoms and molecules (1–100 nm), is transforming medicine by enabling precise, targeted, and minimally invasive healthcare interventions.
mains_body_points: Definition: Manipulation of matter at nanoscale to exploit unique physical, chemical, and biological properties
Drug delivery: Nanoparticles carry drugs directly to diseased cells (e.g., cancer tumours), reducing systemic toxicity and improving efficacy of chemotherapy
Diagnostics: Quantum dots and nano-biosensors enable early disease detection; nanoparticle contrast agents improve MRI and imaging
Wound care: Silver nanoparticle dressings have antimicrobial properties accelerating wound healing
Vaccine development: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) enabled mRNA COVID-19 vaccines — a landmark nanotechnology success
India's initiatives: National Mission on Nano Science and Technology (NanoMission) under DST
mains_conclusion: Nanotechnology is poised to revolutionise healthcare by making treatments more precise, diagnostics more sensitive, and therapeutic interventions less invasive.
contemporary_relevance: Lipid nanoparticles enabled COVID-19 mRNA vaccines — a defining nanotechnology breakthrough.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: The table shows interest payments rising from 27.7% to 38.7% of revenue expenditure — this is the key trend indicating fiscal stress from rising debt burden.
elimination_hint: Interest payments rose from 27.7% (1989-90) to 38.7% (1994-95), indicating the government was spending an increasing proportion of revenue on debt servicing — a sign of fiscal stress. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Rising interest payments as % of revenue expenditure indicate increasing debt burden
Interest payments crowding out productive expenditure is a sign of fiscal unsustainability
India's fiscal consolidation efforts in 1990s followed the 1991 BoP crisis
contrast_with: Interest payments (obligatory) vs capital expenditure (productive)
contemporary_relevance: India's interest payments remain a significant portion of total expenditure, constraining fiscal space.

subject: Environment
factual_hooks: EIA Notification 2020 (draft) proposed post-facto clearance for violations and reduced public consultation
EIA 2006 requires mandatory public hearing for Category A and B projects
Draft EIA 2020 was widely criticised for diluting environmental safeguards
contrast_with: EIA 2006 (robust public consultation) vs Draft EIA 2020 (reduced transparency)
mains_intro: The Draft EIA Notification 2020 proposed significant changes to the existing EIA framework of 2006, sparking debate about the balance between ease of doing business and environmental protection.
mains_body_points: Post-facto clearance: Draft 2020 allowed regularisation of projects that began without prior clearance — EIA 2006 strictly prohibits this
Reduced public consultation: Draft 2020 reduced public notice period from 30 to 20 days and exempted more project categories from public hearings
Strategic projects: Draft 2020 allowed government to classify certain projects as strategic, exempting them from public disclosure — not in 2006 notification
Compliance reporting: EIA 2006 required half-yearly reports; Draft 2020 proposed annual reporting, reducing monitoring frequency
Criticism: Civil society argued Draft 2020 weakened environmental safeguards; proponents said it streamlined business processes
mains_conclusion: The EIA 2020 draft highlighted the tension between development imperatives and environmental governance, underscoring the need for robust, participatory, and precautionary environmental regulation.
mains_interlinkages: Polity: NGT, judicial review of environmental decisions
contemporary_relevance: Draft EIA 2020 was challenged in courts; final revised EIA notification remains pending as of 2024.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may think SBI nationalisation or Insurance nationalisation came first — Banking Regulation Act was enacted in 1949, before SBI nationalisation (1955) and Insurance nationalisation (1956), and before the First Five Year Plan (1951-56).
elimination_hint: Banking Regulation Act 1949 was the earliest; First Five Year Plan began 1951; SBI formed 1955 (nationalising Imperial Bank); Insurance nationalisation 1956. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Banking Regulation Act enacted in 1949
Imperial Bank nationalised as State Bank of India in 1955
First Five Year Plan: 1951-56; Insurance nationalised: 1956
contrast_with: Banking Regulation Act (1949) vs Bank Nationalisation (1969 — 14 banks, 1980 — 6 more)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: BMW is German (Munich), not USA; Daimler is German (Stuttgart), not Swedish; Kookmin Bank is South Korean, not Japanese. Only Renault (France) and Volkswagen (Germany) are correctly matched.
elimination_hint: Only pairs 3 (Renault-France) and 4 (Volkswagen-Germany) are correctly matched. BMW and Daimler are both German; Kookmin Bank is South Korean. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: BMW AG is headquartered in Munich, Germany
Daimler AG (Mercedes-Benz) is in Stuttgart, Germany
Renault is French; Volkswagen is German; Kookmin Bank is South Korean
prerequisite_concepts: Multinational corporations and their home countries

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may calculate weight as 200cc × density of water × g — but a rubber balloon filled with water submerged in water displaces exactly its own weight. Net weight = 0.
elimination_hint: By Archimedes' principle, an object fully submerged in a fluid experiences buoyant force equal to weight of fluid displaced. A water balloon in water displaces its own weight — net weight is zero. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Archimedes' principle: buoyant force = weight of displaced fluid
Water-filled balloon in water: weight of object = buoyant force, so apparent weight = 0
This is why aquatic creatures are effectively weightless in water
contrast_with: Weight in air vs apparent weight in water

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: INS Sindhughosh is a submarine (Kilo-class), not an aircraft carrier. INS Viraat was an aircraft carrier (decommissioned 2017), not a submarine. Both statements are wrong.
elimination_hint: INS Sindhughosh is a Kilo-class submarine; INS Viraat was a Centaur-class aircraft carrier. Both statements are factually reversed — answer is D (neither correct).
factual_hooks: INS Sindhughosh is a Kilo-class (877EKM) submarine
INS Viraat was India's second aircraft carrier (decommissioned 2017)
India's current aircraft carrier: INS Vikrant (commissioned 2022) and INS Vikramaditya
contrast_with: Aircraft carrier (power projection) vs submarine (stealth warfare)
contemporary_relevance: India's aircraft carrier programme (INS Vikrant) represents a major milestone in indigenous defence capability.

subject: Internal Security
factual_hooks: Border area management requires civil-military coordination
Winning 'hearts and minds' in border areas reduces local support for militants
Development-security nexus is key in border area management
contrast_with: Kinetic (force-based) vs non-kinetic (development-based) counter-insurgency
mains_intro: Effective border area management requires a dual approach: denying militants local sanctuary through intelligence and operations, while winning the trust of border communities through development and good governance.
mains_body_points: Denying local support: Intelligence-led operations using HUMINT from local sources; targeted action against militant logistics and overground workers; community policing and Village Defence Committees
Cutting off recruitment: Address grievances — unemployment, ethnic marginalisation — that make youth vulnerable to militant recruitment
Development as security: Road connectivity, health facilities, schools, economic opportunities in border villages reduce alienation
Favourable local perception: Transparent conduct of security forces, accountability for excesses, consistent communication about government schemes and protection
Civil administration: Strengthening civil administration presence in remote border areas through special incentives for posting; gram sabhas and local governance
mains_conclusion: Sustainable border security requires not just military dominance but legitimacy in the eyes of border communities, achieved through integrated development, justice, and responsive governance.
contemporary_relevance: Border area management is critical in J&K, Northeast India, and LWE-affected districts.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Kochi, Paradip, New Mangalore are all Major Ports under Major Port Trusts Act — Dahej is a minor/non-major port in Gujarat.
elimination_hint: Major ports in India: 12 major ports under Ministry of Shipping. Dahej (Gujarat) is a non-major port. Kochi, Paradip, New Mangalore are among the 12 major ports. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: India has 12 Major Ports under Major Port Trusts Act 1963
Non-major ports are under state governments
Dahej is an emerging industrial/GIDC port in Gujarat, not a major port
prerequisite_concepts: Major vs Minor/Non-major ports in India
Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways
contrast_with: Major ports (central/national jurisdiction) vs non-major ports (state jurisdiction)
contemporary_relevance: India's Sagarmala programme aims to modernise both major and non-major ports.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: This is a 2009 current affairs question about Indian industrialists — Anil Agarwal is associated with Vedanta Resources (mining/metals), not Videocon. Venugopal Dhoot is from Videocon.
elimination_hint: Anil Agarwal = Vedanta Resources; Gautam Singhania = Raymond Ltd; Sanjay Dalmia = Gujarat Heavy Chemicals; Venugopal Dhoot = Videocon. However the correct answer given is B (A-4,B-1,C-2,D-3) which doesn't match standard knowledge — this may be a question error.
factual_hooks: Anil Agarwal is the founder-chairman of Vedanta Resources
Venugopal Dhoot is the chairman of Videocon Group
Gautam H. Singhania leads Raymond Ltd
prerequisite_concepts: Indian business groups and their founders

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: Rice-wheat system covers ~13.5 million hectares in Indo-Gangetic Plains
Green Revolution was built on this system — enabled food self-sufficiency
System causes groundwater depletion (Punjab losing water table ~1m/year), soil degradation, stubble burning
contrast_with: Green Revolution success (food security) vs long-term ecological and economic costs
mains_intro: The rice-wheat cropping system of the Indo-Gangetic Plains powered India's Green Revolution but has become an ecological and economic liability threatening long-term agricultural sustainability.
mains_body_points: Success factors: HYV seeds, assured irrigation (canal + groundwater), MSP support, and procurement infrastructure made rice-wheat system highly productive
Contribution: India achieved food self-sufficiency, moved from food aid dependence to net exporter of rice and wheat
Ecological bane: Intensive groundwater use causes alarming depletion (Punjab water table falling at 1m/year); soil health deteriorating from mono-cropping and heavy fertiliser use
Stubble burning: Short turnaround between rice harvest and wheat sowing forces farmers to burn paddy straw, causing severe air pollution in north India
Economic stress: Diminishing returns — yield growth stagnating; high input costs eroding farm profitability
Solutions: Crop diversification (pulses, oilseeds), direct seeding of rice, zero-tillage wheat, Mera Pani Meri Virasat (incentives for crop change)
mains_conclusion: The rice-wheat system must be transformed from an input-intensive monoculture into a diversified, resource-efficient system to balance food security with ecological sustainability.
contemporary_relevance: Punjab's groundwater crisis and Delhi's air pollution from stubble burning make this a pressing current issue.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 says RBI was nationalised on 26 January 1950 — it was nationalised on 1 January 1949 (not Republic Day). Statement 2 says borrowing programme is handled by Dept of Expenditure — it is actually handled by Dept of Economic Affairs. Both are wrong.
elimination_hint: RBI was nationalised on 1 January 1949 (not 26 Jan 1950). Government borrowing programme is managed by Dept of Economic Affairs (Ministry of Finance), not Dept of Expenditure. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: RBI was established in 1935 and nationalised on 1 January 1949
Government borrowing programme is handled by Dept of Economic Affairs, MoF
Dept of Expenditure handles public expenditure management, not borrowing
contrast_with: Dept of Economic Affairs (borrowing, forex, capital markets) vs Dept of Expenditure (budget, public finance management)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Cannes is in France (not Italy as option B states) — this is the incorrectly matched pair being asked about.
elimination_hint: Cannes is in France (not Italy); Cancun is in Mexico; Bruges is in Belgium — the question asks for the incorrectly matched pair, which is B (Cannes-Italy).
factual_hooks: Cannes is in France — famous for the Cannes Film Festival
Cancun is in Mexico — venue of WTO/climate negotiations
Bruges is a historic city in Belgium

subject: Society
trap_factor: Statement 2 says Mizoram has lowest population density — Arunachal Pradesh actually has the lowest population density; Mizoram is not among the lowest two. Statement 3 about Kerala having both highest literacy and sex ratio is correct.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 (Haryana and Punjab lowest sex ratio — 2001) is correct. Statement 2 (Mizoram lowest population density) is wrong — Arunachal Pradesh has lowest. Statement 3 (Kerala highest literacy + sex ratio) is correct. Answer is D (1 and 3).
factual_hooks: As per 2001 Census: Haryana and Punjab had lowest sex ratios among major states
Kerala had highest literacy rate and highest sex ratio in 2001
Lowest population density states: Arunachal Pradesh, followed by Mizoram
contrast_with: Kerala (best human development indicators) vs BIMARU states (poor indicators)
contemporary_relevance: 2011 Census shows improvements in sex ratio but gender gap persists in NW India.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Pankaj Advani is a billiards/snooker player (not badminton); Rohan Bopanna is a tennis player; Ignace Tirkey played hockey; Shikha Tandon is a swimmer.
elimination_hint: Shikha Tandon-Swimming, Ignace Tirkey-Hockey, Pankaj Advani-Snooker, Rohan Bopanna-Tennis. Match: A-2, B-5, C-4, D-3. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Pankaj Advani is a renowned billiards and snooker player
Rohan Bopanna is an Indian tennis doubles specialist
Ignace Tirkey played hockey for India

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may struggle with the diminishing workforce — need to compute work done in each 10-day block as workers reduce, then sum up.
elimination_hint: Total work = 50×50 = 2500 man-days. Block 1 (days 1-10): 40 men + 48 women; compute equivalent men, multiply by 10. Continue until cumulative work = 2500. Answer is B (50 days).
factual_hooks: Work-time problems: Total work = men × days
Efficiency ratio: 50 men = 80 women → 1 man = 1.6 women
Track cumulative work in each block as workforce reduces

subject: Environment
factual_hooks: India joined the Coalition for Disaster Resilient Infrastructure (CDRI) in 2019
NDMA formulates disaster management policies under DM Act 2005
Sendai Framework 2015-2030 guides India's disaster risk reduction strategy
contrast_with: Pre-disaster resilience building vs post-disaster response
mains_intro: India has moved from a reactive disaster response approach to proactive disaster risk reduction, with resilient infrastructure at the centre of its strategy.
mains_body_points: Coalition for Disaster Resilient Infrastructure (CDRI): India launched CDRI at UNGA 2019 — a global partnership to promote resilient infrastructure in developing nations
National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA): Formulates guidelines for disaster resilient construction — earthquake-resistant buildings, flood-proof roads, cyclone shelters
PMGSY and rural roads: Standards upgraded for resilience against floods and landslides
Cyclone resilient infrastructure: Gujarat and Odisha models — reinforced pucca houses, early warning systems reduced cyclone deaths dramatically
Sendai Framework alignment: India's National DRR Plan 2019-2030 aligns with Sendai's priorities: understand risk, strengthen governance, invest in resilience
Smart Cities Mission: Incorporates climate resilience and disaster risk reduction in urban infrastructure planning
mains_conclusion: India's leadership in CDRI, combined with domestic policy reforms, positions it as a global champion for disaster resilient infrastructure, though implementation gaps at sub-national level remain.
contemporary_relevance: CDRI's importance grew after COVID-19 highlighted need for resilient health infrastructure; India also chairs CDRI.

subject: History
factual_hooks: Integration of 565 princely states was achieved through instruments of accession, persuasion, and coercion
Sardar Patel and V.P. Menon orchestrated the integration
Hyderabad (Operation Polo 1948) and Kashmir required military action; Junagadh had referendum
contrast_with: Peaceful accession vs coercive integration (Hyderabad, Junagadh)
mains_intro: The integration of 565 princely states into the Indian Union between 1947-50 was one of the most complex administrative and political exercises in modern history, accompanied by significant administrative and socio-cultural challenges.
mains_body_points: Administrative issues: Disparate administrative systems across princely states required standardisation; varying civil service traditions, law codes, and taxation systems needed harmonisation
Resistance to central authority: Some states like Hyderabad, Junagadh, and Kashmir resisted accession, requiring diplomatic pressure or military action (Operation Polo for Hyderabad)
Revenue and financial integration: Many states had independent currencies and tariff regimes that needed to be absorbed into the Indian financial system
Socio-cultural problems: Integration threatened traditional ruling class privileges; feudal agrarian structures (jagirdari, zamindari) needed dismantling
Linguistic and ethnic tensions: Many integrated states had distinct cultural identities leading to demands for reorganisation along linguistic lines — culminating in States Reorganisation Act 1956
mains_conclusion: The successful but complex integration of princely states laid the foundation of India's federal structure, though its socio-cultural legacy — of linguistic reorganisation and regional identities — continues to shape Indian politics.
contemporary_relevance: The integration process informs current debates on special status provisions and centre-state relations.

subject: Geography
factual_hooks: Himalayan landslides are triggered by steep slopes, seismic activity, heavy monsoon, deforestation, and construction activity
Western Ghats landslides are driven by intense rainfall, laterite soil on steep slopes, and loss of forest cover
Himalayan region has younger, unstable geology vs Western Ghats' older, more stable but heavily eroded terrain
contrast_with: Himalayan (seismic + steep + rain) vs Western Ghats (intense rain + laterite + human pressure) landslide causes
mains_intro: Landslides in the Himalayan region and Western Ghats share the trigger of heavy rainfall but differ fundamentally in their geological, tectonic, and anthropogenic causes.
mains_body_points: Himalayan causes: (i) Younger, fragile geology — rocks are still undergoing orogeny and are structurally weak; (ii) High seismic activity in zone IV and V triggers slope failures; (iii) Steep gradients (3000-7000m altitude) combined with heavy monsoon and glacial melt; (iv) Unplanned construction of roads (e.g., Char Dham highway cutting slopes), tourism pressure, deforestation

Western Ghats causes: (i) Intense, concentrated rainfall — some of the highest in India (Cherrapunji-equivalent zones like Wayanad, Munnar); (ii) Laterite soils are prone to slippage when saturated; (iii) Deforestation and monoculture plantations (tea, rubber) replacing natural forests reduce root binding; (iv) No major seismic trigger — geology is older and more stable
Common factors: Both regions see intensification due to climate change increasing extreme rainfall events and human encroachment into fragile slopes
mains_conclusion: Landslide risk reduction in both regions requires region-specific approaches — seismic risk mitigation and regulated infrastructure in the Himalayas, and forest cover restoration and drainage management in the Western Ghats.
contemporary_relevance: Wayanad landslide (2024) and Joshimath subsidence highlight the urgency of addressing landslide risks in both regions.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may guess the B.K. Chaturvedi Committee dealt with Centre-State relations or tax reforms — it was specifically about petroleum product pricing reforms.
elimination_hint: B.K. Chaturvedi Committee (2007-08) examined pricing of petroleum products and the petroleum subsidy mechanism — specifically oil sector price reforms. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: B.K. Chaturvedi Committee (2007) examined pricing of petroleum products in India
It recommended reforms to reduce oil subsidies and move towards market-based pricing
Another Chaturvedi committee examined restructuring of centrally sponsored schemes
contrast_with: Administered pricing mechanism (APM) vs market-determined petroleum prices

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Mahamastakabhisheka with a Buddhist or Hindu ceremony — it is the grand anointing ceremony specific to the Jain deity Bahubali (Gomateshwara) at Shravanabelagola.
elimination_hint: Mahamastakabhisheka is the Jain head-anointing ceremony for Bahubali's statue at Shravanabelagola (Karnataka), held once every 12 years. Answer is A.
factual_hooks: Mahamastakabhisheka is the anointing ceremony of Gomateshwara (Bahubali) at Shravanabelagola
Held once every 12 years — one of the largest religious gatherings in India
Bahubali was the son of Rishabhanatha (first Jain Tirthankara)
contrast_with: Jain ceremony vs Hindu Kumbh Mela vs Buddhist ceremonies

subject: History
trap_factor: Gatka is a traditional martial art of Punjab/Sikhs — not Kerala. Pair 1 (Gatka-Kerala) is wrong. Madhubani-Bihar and Singhwy Khababs/Sindhu Darshan-J&K are correct.
elimination_hint: Gatka is from Punjab (Sikh martial art), not Kerala. Madhubani is a painting tradition from Mithila/Bihar (correct). Sindhu Darshan Festival is in J&K/Ladakh (correct). Answer is C (pairs 2 and 3).
factual_hooks: Gatka is the traditional Sikh martial art from Punjab
Madhubani/Mithila painting is from Bihar — UNESCO recognised tradition
Sindhu Darshan Festival is held at Leh, Ladakh on the banks of Indus
prerequisite_concepts: Regional art and cultural traditions of India
contrast_with: Gatka (Punjab) vs Kalaripayattu (Kerala) — two different regional martial arts

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Rukmini Devi Arundale (revivalist of Bharatanatyam) with a singer, or Bhimsen Joshi (vocalist) with a tabla player.
elimination_hint: Amrita Sher-Gil-Painter; Bhimsen Joshi-Singer (Kirana Gharana vocalist); Rukmini Devi Arundale-Dancer (revived Bharatanatyam); Suryakant Tripathi Nirala-Poet (Hindi Chhayavad). Answer is A (A-2, B-1, C-4 is wrong; correct is A-2, B-4, C-1, D-3) — wait, answer given is A which maps A-2, B-1, C-4, D-3 which doesn't match standard knowledge.
factual_hooks: Amrita Sher-Gil is one of India's greatest painters (Indo-Hungarian)
Pandit Bhimsen Joshi was a legendary Hindustani classical vocalist (Kirana Gharana)
Rukmini Devi Arundale revived Bharatanatyam at Kalakshetra, Chennai
Suryakant Tripathi Nirala was a major Hindi Chhayavad poet
prerequisite_concepts: Indian cultural personalities and their fields

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojana (RSBY) was launched by the Ministry of Labour and Employment — NOT Ministry of Health. The Rajiv Gandhi Shilpi Swasthya Bima Yojana was by Ministry of Textiles (correct).
elimination_hint: RSBY was launched by Ministry of Labour and Employment (2008) for BPL families — not Health Ministry. Statement 1 is wrong; Statement 2 (Rajiv Gandhi Shilpi-Textiles) is correct. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojana (RSBY) was under Ministry of Labour and Employment
Later RSBY was subsumed under Ayushman Bharat (PM-JAY) in 2018
Rajiv Gandhi Shilpi Swasthya Bima Yojana was for handloom/handicraft artisans under Textiles Ministry
contrast_with: RSBY (Labour Ministry) vs CGHS (Health Ministry) vs PM-JAY (Health Ministry)
contemporary_relevance: RSBY's successor PM-JAY is the world's largest health assurance scheme covering 500 million people.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Students may confuse Amartya Sen's work 'In Custody' with Anita Desai — Amartya Sen wrote 'The Argumentative Indian'; 'In Custody' is by Anita Desai.
elimination_hint: In Custody-Anita Desai; Sea of Poppies-Amitav Ghosh; The Argumentative Indian-Amartya Sen; Unaccustomed Earth-Jhumpa Lahiri. Match: A-3, B-2, C-1, D-4. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: 'In Custody' (1984) by Anita Desai was also filmed by Ismail Merchant
'Sea of Poppies' by Amitav Ghosh is first in the Ibis trilogy
'The Argumentative Indian' (2005) by Amartya Sen explores Indian intellectual traditions
'Unaccustomed Earth' (2008) by Jhumpa Lahiri won multiple literary awards

subject: Society
trap_factor: Agaria sounds like it could be an agricultural or toddy-tapper community — they are actually traditional iron smelters of Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh, but the question relates to salt pan workers of Gujarat.
elimination_hint: Agaria community of Gujarat are traditional salt pan workers (working in Rann of Kutch salt flats). This is distinct from the Agaria ironsmith community of Chhattisgarh. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Agaria in Gujarat context = traditional salt pan workers of Rann of Kutch
They face extreme conditions working in salt deserts
A different Agaria community in MP/Chhattisgarh are traditional iron smelters
contrast_with: Agaria salt workers (Gujarat) vs Agaria iron smelters (Chhattisgarh) — same name, different groups
contemporary_relevance: Agaria salt workers' working conditions and land rights in Rann of Kutch have been subject to policy advocacy.

subject: History
trap_factor: Dilwara Temple is in Mount Abu but is not a UNESCO World Heritage Site; Bhiterkanika is a Ramsar site but not UNESCO WHS. Kalka-Shimla Railway was added to UNESCO Mountain Railways of India site.
elimination_hint: In 2008, Kalka-Shimla Railway was added to the UNESCO World Heritage Site 'Mountain Railways of India' (which includes Darjeeling Himalayan Railway and Nilgiri Mountain Railway). Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Mountain Railways of India (UNESCO WHS) includes Darjeeling, Nilgiri, and Kalka-Shimla railways
Kalka-Shimla Railway was added to this UNESCO site in 2008
Dilwara Temple and Bhiterkanika are not UNESCO WHSs
prerequisite_concepts: UNESCO World Heritage Sites in India
contrast_with: UNESCO WHS vs Ramsar Sites vs Biosphere Reserves
contemporary_relevance: India currently has 42 UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Virupaksha temple with Chidambaram (Nataraja) or Srikalahasti (famous Shiva temple) — Virupaksha at Hampi is a Shiva temple that was the royal temple of the Vijayanagara Empire.
elimination_hint: Virupaksha temple is at Hampi (Karnataka) — it was the royal temple of Vijayanagara Empire. Hampi is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
factual_hooks: Virupaksha Temple is located at Hampi, Karnataka
It was the principal temple of the Vijayanagara Empire (14th-16th century CE)
Hampi is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
contrast_with: Virupaksha at Hampi vs Virupaksha at Pattadakal (also in Karnataka — Chalukyan)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may guess Ministry of Science and Technology or Ministry of Power for atomic energy — Department of Atomic Energy is directly under the Prime Minister's Office.
elimination_hint: Department of Atomic Energy (DAE) is directly under the Prime Minister's Office — a deliberate policy decision for strategic oversight of nuclear programme. Answer is A.
factual_hooks: Department of Atomic Energy is directly under the Prime Minister's Office
This reflects the strategic importance of nuclear policy
DAE oversees BARC, NPCIL, ISRO (was under DAE earlier), and other nuclear organisations
contrast_with: DAE (PMO) vs Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (separate ministry)
contemporary_relevance: India's nuclear expansion (10 new reactors approved) makes DAE's policy role increasingly significant.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: ABN Amro is a Dutch (Netherlands) bank, not American. Kookmin Bank is South Korean, not Japanese. Only Barclays (UK) is correctly matched.
elimination_hint: ABN Amro Bank is headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands (not USA). Kookmin Bank is South Korea (not Japan). Only Barclays Bank-UK (pair 2) is correct. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: ABN Amro Bank: Dutch bank (Amsterdam, Netherlands)
Barclays Bank: British bank (London, UK)
Kookmin Bank: South Korean bank (Seoul)

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may guess Rajendra Prasad or Patel as second Satyagrahi — Nehru was chosen as second to emphasise the political significance of the Individual Satyagraha (1940-41) against WWII participation.
elimination_hint: In Individual Satyagraha (1940): Vinoba Bhave was first; Jawaharlal Nehru was second — chosen by Gandhi to signal the national importance of the campaign. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Individual Satyagraha launched by Gandhi in 1940 against British war effort in WWII
Vinoba Bhave was chosen first; Nehru was second
The movement was limited, symbolic protest — not mass civil disobedience
contrast_with: Individual Satyagraha (1940, limited) vs Quit India (1942, mass movement)

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may think Cripps Mission offered full independence — it offered Dominion Status (not full independence) after WWII; it did provide for a Constitution-making body. Statement 1 (full independence) is wrong.
elimination_hint: Cripps Proposals (1942): offered Dominion Status (not full independence) after WWII; did provide for a Constituent Assembly to frame Constitution. Statement 2 is correct, Statement 1 is wrong. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Cripps Mission (1942) offered Dominion Status after WWII — not full independence
It proposed creation of a Constitution-making body with elected representatives
Both Congress and Muslim League rejected Cripps Proposals for different reasons
contrast_with: Cripps offer (Dominion Status) vs actual independence (1947 — full sovereignty)

subject: History
trap_factor: Aruna Asaf Ali is associated with multiple movements — her most famous contribution was hoisting the INC flag at Gowalia Tank (Bombay) during the Quit India Movement, not Civil Disobedience or Non-Cooperation.
elimination_hint: Aruna Asaf Ali became famous for hoisting the Congress flag at Gowalia Tank, Bombay on 9 August 1942 (Quit India launch) and organising underground resistance — answer is C.
factual_hooks: Aruna Asaf Ali hoisted INC flag at Gowalia Tank on 9 August 1942
She organised underground revolutionary activities during Quit India Movement
Known as the 'Grand Old Lady of the Independence Movement'
contrast_with: Quit India (1942, mass and underground) vs Civil Disobedience (1930-31, non-violent mass protest)

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may guess Akbar (who interacted with Portuguese) — but Surat factory was established in 1608-13 under Jahangir's reign with Thomas Best's naval victory convincing Jahangir.
elimination_hint: English East India Company established their first factory at Surat in 1608-1613 during Jahangir's reign, after Thomas Best's naval victory over the Portuguese impressed Mughal officials. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: English East India Company's first factory at Surat established 1608-13 under Jahangir
Captain Thomas Best's defeat of Portuguese navy at Battle of Swally (1612) convinced Jahangir
First imperial firman for trade was granted by Jahangir
related_concepts: Portuguese vs English rivalry in India
contrast_with: Portuguese (16th century) vs English (17th century) arrival in India

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may guess sodium or calcium-based electrolytes — dry cells use ammonium chloride (as electrolyte paste) and zinc chloride (to reduce polarisation).
elimination_hint: A dry cell (Leclanché cell) uses ammonium chloride and zinc chloride as electrolytes in paste form. Answer is A.
factual_hooks: Dry cell (Leclanché cell) electrolyte: ammonium chloride + zinc chloride in paste
Zinc casing acts as anode; carbon rod is cathode
Called 'dry' because electrolyte is in paste, not liquid form
contrast_with: Dry cell (ammonium/zinc chloride) vs alkaline cell (potassium hydroxide)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may think jasmine uses grafting (like other ornamental plants) — jasmine uses layering, while citrus/sweet orange uses grafting. Both statements are correct.
elimination_hint: Sweet orange is propagated by budding/grafting (vegetative propagation preserving fruit quality). Jasmine propagates by stem layering. Both statements 1 and 2 are correct. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Grafting in citrus preserves desirable fruit characteristics (seedless, taste)
Jasmine propagates by layering — bending a stem to touch soil until roots form
Vegetative propagation maintains genetic consistency
contrast_with: Sexual (seed) propagation vs vegetative propagation methods

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may confuse night vision with radar (microwaves) — night vision devices detect heat (infrared/thermal radiation) emitted by objects in the dark.
elimination_hint: Night vision apparatus detects infrared radiation (heat) emitted by objects. Objects emit IR proportional to their temperature — this is used to create thermal images in darkness. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Night vision devices use infrared (thermal) radiation detection
All objects above absolute zero emit IR radiation
Active night vision uses IR illuminator; passive uses ambient IR/thermal imaging
contrast_with: Active night vision (IR illuminator) vs passive thermal imaging
contemporary_relevance: Night vision is critical for military and border security operations.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may think the daughter also gets colour blindness — but the daughter gets one X-chromosome from colour-blind mother (X^c) and one normal X from father (X^N), making her a carrier, not colour blind. The son gets X^c from mother and Y from father, making him colour blind.
elimination_hint: Colour blindness is X-linked recessive. Mother (X^c X^c) passes X^c to both children. Father (X^N Y) gives X^N to daughter and Y to son. Son: X^c Y = colour blind. Daughter: X^c X^N = carrier, not colour blind. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Colour blindness is X-linked recessive trait
Affected mother (X^cX^c) passes defective allele to all children
Sons who get X^c from mother will be colour blind; daughters become carriers
contrast_with: X-linked recessive vs autosomal recessive inheritance patterns

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may confuse phosphorus trichloride (used in chemical weapons) with explosives — Nitroglycerine is the well-known powerful explosive used in dynamite.
elimination_hint: Nitroglycerine (glyceryl trinitrate) is a powerful explosive and also a vasodilator used in medicine. It is the active component of Alfred Nobel's dynamite. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Nitroglycerine is an unstable powerful explosive — Nobel invented dynamite by stabilising it with diatomite
Also used medically as vasodilator for angina treatment
PCl3 is a chemical weapon precursor, not an explosive
contrast_with: Low explosives (gunpowder) vs high explosives (nitroglycerine, TNT)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 3 says no chemical fertilisers at all — SSI does allow some fertiliser use (it promotes organic + judicious chemical use, not zero chemicals). Statement 3 is wrong; statements 1, 2, 4 are correct.
elimination_hint: Sustainable Sugarcane Initiative (SSI): uses single-bud seedlings (very low seed cost), promotes drip irrigation, allows intercropping, but does NOT completely eliminate chemical fertilisers. Answer is B (1, 2, 4 only).
factual_hooks: SSI uses single-bud seedlings reducing seed cost by 85-90% vs conventional method
SSI promotes drip/micro-irrigation for water efficiency
Wide spacing in SSI increases scope for intercropping
contrast_with: SSI (resource-efficient) vs conventional sugarcane farming (input-intensive)
contemporary_relevance: SSI is being promoted as a sustainable alternative given water stress in sugarcane-growing regions.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may think vanilla is synthetic — natural vanilla comes from Vanilla planifolia (an orchid). Camphor comes from camphor tree; Chicory from Cichorium intybus plant. All three are plant products.
elimination_hint: Camphor is derived from camphor tree (Cinnamomum camphora); Chicory is from Cichorium intybus root; Vanilla bean is the fruit of Vanilla planifolia orchid. All three are plant products. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Camphor from Cinnamomum camphora bark by steam distillation
Chicory roots are used as coffee substitute/additive
Natural vanilla extracted from Vanilla planifolia seed pods — world's most popular flavour
contrast_with: Natural vanilla vs synthetic vanillin (most vanilla flavour today is synthetic)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Flying fox sounds like a bird (kite, stork) due to its name — it is actually a large fruit bat (megabat) that is an important pollinator and seed disperser.
elimination_hint: Flying fox (Pteropus genus) is the world's largest bat — a frugivorous megabat important for forest regeneration through seed dispersal and pollination. Answer is A.
factual_hooks: Flying fox is a large fruit bat (Pteropus), not a fox or bird
Important pollinator and seed disperser for forest ecosystems
Indian Flying Fox (Pteropus giganteus) is found across South Asia
contrast_with: Flying fox (megabat) vs insectivorous bats (microbats)
contemporary_relevance: Bats including flying fox play a crucial role in ecosystem services; they are also studied as reservoir hosts for viruses.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may think Montreux Record relates to heritage status or law-based prohibition — it simply flags wetlands where ecological character has changed or is threatened due to human activities.
elimination_hint: Montreux Record is a register under Ramsar Convention for wetlands facing adverse ecological changes due to human interference. Answer is A.
factual_hooks: Montreux Record: list of Ramsar wetlands where ecological character has changed/is threatened due to human interference
India's Loktak Lake and Keoladeo National Park were on Montreux Record (later removed)
It doesn't grant WHS status or impose legal prohibitions
contrast_with: Montreux Record (threatened Ramsar sites) vs UNESCO Ramsar designation (all listed wetlands)
contemporary_relevance: India has the most Ramsar sites in Asia (75+ sites); monitoring their ecological health is critical.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: All three programmes sound correctly matched — but all three are mismatched. DPAP is under Ministry of Rural Development (not Agriculture); Desert Development Programme is under Ministry of Rural Development (not Environment); NWDPRA is under Ministry of Agriculture (not Rural Development).
elimination_hint: All three are wrongly matched: DPAP and DDP are under Ministry of Rural Development; NWDPRA is under Ministry of Agriculture. Answer is D (none correctly matched).
factual_hooks: Drought-Prone Area Programme (DPAP): Ministry of Rural Development
Desert Development Programme (DDP): Ministry of Rural Development
National Watershed Development Project for Rainfed Areas (NWDPRA): Ministry of Agriculture
contrast_with: Rural development ministry (area development) vs Agriculture ministry (farm productivity)
contemporary_relevance: Watershed programmes are now integrated under PM Krishi Sinchayee Yojana.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 1 says BNHS is under Ministry of Environment — BNHS is NOT an autonomous government organisation; it is an independent NGO (though it collaborates with government). Statement 1 is wrong.
elimination_hint: BNHS (Bombay Natural History Society) is an independent NGO, not an autonomous body under MoEF. Statements 2 (conservation through research) and 3 (nature trails and camps) are correct. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: BNHS is an independent conservation NGO founded in 1883
It conducts action-based research and public awareness on nature conservation
BNHS is not under Ministry of Environment — it is an autonomous civil society organisation
contrast_with: Government conservation bodies (WII, ZSI) vs NGOs (BNHS, WWF)
contemporary_relevance: BNHS plays a crucial role in bird migration studies and conservation advocacy in India.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may think GEF undertakes scientific research or is an OECD body — GEF is a financial mechanism for multiple environmental conventions, not a research body and not under OECD.
elimination_hint: GEF serves as the financial mechanism for CBD, UNFCCC, UNCCD, and other conventions. It is not an OECD body and does not itself conduct scientific research. Answer is A.
factual_hooks: Global Environment Facility (GEF) is the financial mechanism for CBD, UNFCCC, UNCCD, and Stockholm Convention
Established in 1991; headquartered in Washington DC
GEF channels funds from developed to developing countries for global environmental projects
contrast_with: GEF (financial mechanism for multiple conventions) vs Green Climate Fund (only climate) vs Adaptation Fund
contemporary_relevance: GEF is a critical funding source for India's biodiversity and climate projects.

subject: Science & Technology
factual_hooks: Blue LEDs (Nobel 2014) enabled white LED light by combining with yellow phosphor
LEDs use 75-90% less energy than incandescent bulbs
Blue LEDs enabled energy-efficient displays in smartphones, TVs, and monitors
contrast_with: Incandescent bulb (5% efficient) vs LED (>50% efficient) for lighting
mains_themes: Science and technology in everyday life
mains_intro: The invention of blue LEDs by Akasaki, Amano, and Nakamura in the 1990s completed the RGB LED spectrum, enabling the creation of white light and revolutionising lighting and display technologies globally.
mains_body_points: Lighting revolution: Blue LED + yellow phosphor = white LED; LED bulbs use 75-90% less energy than incandescent bulbs and last 25x longer
Energy savings: Global switch to LED lighting could save energy equivalent to hundreds of power plants — critical for climate mitigation
Displays and screens: Blue LED enabled full-colour LED displays in smartphones, TVs, monitors, and outdoor advertising; foundation of OLED technology
Internet infrastructure: Blue laser diodes enabled higher data density Blu-ray discs and optical fibre communications
Medical applications: LED-based phototherapy for neonatal jaundice, dermatology, and surgical illumination
India's LED push: UJALA scheme distributes LED bulbs — India replaced over 370 million incandescent bulbs saving massive electricity
mains_conclusion: Blue LED invention stands as one of the 21st century's most impactful scientific breakthroughs, simultaneously addressing energy poverty, climate change, and expanding human connectivity through advanced displays and communications.
mains_interlinkages: Environment: energy efficiency and carbon reduction
contemporary_relevance: LED technology is central to India's UJALA scheme, which has avoided millions of tonnes of CO2 emissions.

subject: History
trap_factor: The Emergency (1975-77) was clamped during the Fifth Five Year Plan (1974-79) — students may guess Fourth Plan or Sixth Plan.
elimination_hint: Fifth Five Year Plan (1974-79): Emergency clamped June 1975; elections held March 1977; Janata Party won — all within the 5th FYP period. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Fifth Five Year Plan: 1974-79; Emergency: June 1975 – March 1977
Janata Party won 1977 elections — first non-Congress government at Centre
Sixth Plan (1980-85) saw return of Indira Gandhi
contrast_with: Fourth Plan (1969-74) vs Fifth Plan (1974-79, Emergency) vs Sixth Plan (1980-85)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Students may think Italy was not in original G-7 — Italy was a founding member. Russia joined to make G-8 in 1997; Russia was later suspended in 2014 (Crimea annexation), reverting to G-7.
elimination_hint: Original G-7 (1975): USA, UK, France, Germany, Japan, Italy, Canada. Russia joined in 1997 (making G-8). Russia was not one of the original G-7. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: G-7 was formed in 1975 (Rambouillet Summit) with 6 members; Canada joined making it G-7 in 1976
Russia joined in 1997 making G-8; suspended after 2014 Crimea annexation — reverted to G-7
Original members: USA, UK, France, West Germany, Japan, Italy, Canada
contrast_with: G-7 (advanced economies) vs G-20 (broader forum including emerging economies)
contemporary_relevance: G-7 remains important; India was invited as guest at G-7 Hiroshima 2023.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Pamela Jelimo is a Kenyan 800m runner — students may not know this and guess weightlifting. The question asks which is NOT correctly matched — Pamela Jelimo is a runner (middle-distance), not a weightlifter.
elimination_hint: Barbora Spotakova-Javelin (correct); Pamela Jelimo-800m/sprint running, NOT weightlifting (incorrect pair); Sanya Richards-Sprint (correct); Yelena Isinbayeva-Pole Vault (correct). Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Pamela Jelimo (Kenya) won Olympic 800m gold (Beijing 2008)
Yelena Isinbayeva is legendary Russian pole vaulter (multiple world records)
Barbora Spotakova is Czech javelin thrower (Olympic champion)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may guess China as the country that recently (2007-08) launched a moon impact probe — Japan launched SELENE/Kaguya in 2007 which impacted the Moon; China's Chang'e-1 also impacted in 2009 but Japan was earlier.
elimination_hint: Chandrayaan-1's Moon Impact Probe landed Nov 2008. Japan's SELENE (Kaguya) impacted the Moon in June 2009 — but Japan launched SELENE in September 2007 and was the most recent probe before India's MIP. Answer is D (Japan).
factual_hooks: India's Chandrayaan-1 carried Moon Impact Probe (MIP) — November 2008
Japan's SELENE/Kaguya spacecraft was in lunar orbit from 2007
China's Chang'e-1 also orbited/impacted Moon (2007-09)
contrast_with: India's MIP (2008) vs Japan's SELENE (2007) vs China's Chang'e (2007)
contemporary_relevance: India's Chandrayaan-3 successfully landed near lunar south pole in 2023, making India the 4th nation to soft-land on the Moon.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Davos is in Switzerland (not Denmark); Roland Garros is in Paris, France (not Netherlands). Only Cannes-France is correctly matched.
elimination_hint: Cannes (film festival) is in France — correct. Davos is in Switzerland — not Denmark (incorrect). Roland Garros is in Paris, France — not Netherlands (incorrect). Answer is A (only pair 1).
factual_hooks: Cannes Film Festival held at Cannes, French Riviera, France
World Economic Forum annual meeting held at Davos, Switzerland
Roland Garros (French Open tennis) is in Paris, France

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Monte Carlo is a neighbourhood in Monaco, famous for the Grand Prix motor race and casino — not a football club. Arsenal, Aston Villa, Chelsea are all English Premier League clubs.
elimination_hint: Arsenal, Aston Villa, Chelsea are all English football clubs. Monte Carlo is a location in Monaco associated with Formula 1 Grand Prix, not a football club. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Arsenal, Aston Villa, Chelsea are founding members of English Premier League
Monte Carlo is in Monaco — venue of Formula 1 Monaco Grand Prix and a casino resort
Not to be confused with AS Monaco (football club from Monaco)
contrast_with: Football clubs (Arsenal/Chelsea) vs motorsport venue (Monte Carlo)

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: Land reforms most effective in West Bengal (Operation Barga), Kerala (land ceiling acts), and Jammu & Kashmir
Redistribution of land to marginal/small farmers improved agricultural productivity and reduced exploitation
Land reforms reduced dependence on moneylenders and absentee landlords
contrast_with: Successful land reform states (Kerala, WB, J&K) vs less successful (UP, Bihar)
mains_intro: Land reforms implemented in post-independence India aimed to dismantle feudal agrarian structures and improve the socio-economic conditions of marginal and small farmers, with varying degrees of success across states.
mains_body_points: Zamindari abolition: Removed intermediaries between state and cultivators, allowing farmers to pay land revenue directly; benefited tenants by conferring ownership rights
Tenancy reforms: Protected sharecroppers/tenants from eviction and fixed fair rents — West Bengal's Operation Barga registered sharecroppers and guaranteed them 75% of produce
Land ceiling legislation: Imposed upper limits on landholding; surplus land redistributed to landless and marginal farmers — provided productive asset to poorest
Kerala model: Comprehensive land reforms (Kerala Land Reforms Act 1963) redistributed land reducing rural inequality significantly; improved food security of small farmers
Economic impacts: Land ownership improved farmer's access to institutional credit; incentivised investment in land improvement; reduced rural indebtedness
Limitations: Land ceiling exemptions (trusts, partnerships), benami transfers, and inadequate implementation diluted impact in many states; Bihar and UP reforms were weak
mains_conclusion: Where sincerely implemented, land reforms significantly improved socio-economic conditions of small and marginal farmers by converting them from exploited tenants to empowered landowners — though uneven implementation across India remains a challenge.
mains_interlinkages: Society: rural inequality, caste and land relations
contemporary_relevance: Land rights remain unresolved for millions — land record digitisation (DILRMP) and agricultural tenancy reforms are ongoing policy imperatives.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Dugong lives in sea and students may mistake it for a fish or shark — it is a marine mammal (order Sirenia), the only herbivorous marine mammal feeding on sea grass.
elimination_hint: Dugong (Dugong dugon) is a marine mammal — a sirenian (sea cow) related to manatees. It breathes air, gives birth to live young, and feeds on sea grass. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Dugong is a marine mammal (order Sirenia), not a fish or reptile
It is Vulnerable on IUCN Red List
India declared dugong as state animal of Andaman & Nicobar Islands; conservation reserve exists in Gulf of Mannar and Andaman Sea
contrast_with: Dugong (herbivorous marine mammal) vs dolphin (carnivorous marine mammal) vs sea turtle (reptile)
contemporary_relevance: India's Dugong Conservation Reserve in Tamil Nadu is a flagship marine conservation effort.

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Apabhramsa with Sanskrit linguistic deviance or outcastes — Apabhramsa refers to the transitional languages between Prakrit and modern Indo-Aryan languages.
elimination_hint: Apabhramsa (literally 'corrupt' or 'fallen') refers to the late forms of Prakrit dialects that were precursors to modern Indian languages like Hindi, Bengali, Gujarati. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Apabhramsa = transitional language forms between Prakrit and modern Indo-Aryan languages (c. 600-1200 CE)
Early Hindi, Bengali, Marathi, Gujarati evolved from Apabhramsa dialects
Apabhramsa literature includes works of Nathpanthi saints and Jain poets
contrast_with: Sanskrit (classical) vs Prakrit (middle Indo-Aryan) vs Apabhramsa (transitional) vs modern languages

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may confuse Dul Hasti with Beas or Ravi projects — Dul Hasti Power Station is on the Chenab River in Jammu & Kashmir.
elimination_hint: Dul Hasti Power Station (390 MW run-of-river) is on the Chenab River in Kishtwar district, J&K. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Dul Hasti Hydroelectric Project is on the Chenab River, J&K
Installed capacity: 390 MW
Part of India's J&K hydropower development under Indus Waters Treaty provisions
contrast_with: Chenab projects (Baglihar, Dul Hasti) vs Beas projects (BBMB)
contemporary_relevance: J&K hydropower projects on Chenab and Jhelum face Pakistan's objections under Indus Waters Treaty.
